items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils often nutrient-poor despite abundant vegetation?
items.correct: Rapid decomposition and uptake mean nutrients cycle quickly through living biomass, not soil.
items.misconception: Rainforests have rich soils because they are productive.
items.random_wrong: Rainforests are deserts most of the year.
items.why: Students assume productive = rich soil. Actually most nutrients in rainforests are in living biomass; rapid uptake leaves soils thin. That's why clear-cutting destroys long-term productivity quickly.

items.q: Why is ice-albedo feedback a positive feedback loop?
items.correct: Less ice means less reflective surface, so more sunlight is absorbed, causing more warming and more melting.
items.misconception: Because warming is positive (a good thing).
items.random_wrong: Because polar bears help spread heat.
items.why: Students confuse 'positive' (mathematics) with 'good' (everyday). Positive feedback amplifies the change, in any direction - a negative outcome can result from a positive feedback loop.

items.q: What is the difference between in-situ and ex-situ conservation?
items.correct: In-situ protects species in their natural habitat; ex-situ removes them to controlled settings like zoos or seed banks.
items.misconception: They are alternative names for the same approach.
items.random_wrong: One is for plants, the other for animals.
items.why: Students use the terms interchangeably. Each has trade-offs: in-situ preserves ecosystems; ex-situ provides safety net but loses evolutionary context. Most conservation strategies combine both.

items.q: BedZED is located in which UK area?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sutton, London. “Manchester” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Curitiba is famous for which sustainable transport innovation?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bus rapid transit. “Maglev train” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Urban heat islands typically make cities how many degrees Celsius warmer (upper end)?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
